content:
# Ruth Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 881-882 | **Source Image:** RUT_003_page_001.jpg, RUT_003_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Naomi, her mother-in-law, said to her, "My daughter, shall I not seek rest for you, that it may be well with you?

**2** And now, is not Boaz our kinsman, with whose young women you have been? Behold, he is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.

**3** Therefore wash yourself and anoint yourself, and put on your garments and go down to the threshing floor. Do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking.

**4** And it shall be, when he lies down, that you shall mark the place where he lies, and you shall go in and uncover his feet and lie down. And he will tell you what you should do."

**5** And she said to her, "All that you say to me I will do."

**6** And she went down to the threshing floor and did according to all that her mother-in-law had commanded her.

**7** And Boaz ate and drank, and his heart was merry. And he went to lie down at the end of the heap of grain. And she came softly and uncovered his feet and lay down.

**8** And it came to pass at midnight that the man trembled and turned himself, and behold, a woman was lying at his feet.

**9** And he said, "Who are you?" And she said, "I am Ruth, your maidservant. Spread your wing over your maidservant, for you are a kinsman-redeemer."

**10** And he said, "Blessed are you of the LORD, my daughter. You have made your latter act of faithful love greater than the first, in that you have not gone after the young men, whether poor or rich.

**11** And now, my daughter, do not fear. All that you say I will do for you, for all the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of worth.

**12** And now, it is true that I am a kinsman-redeemer, yet there is a kinsman-redeemer nearer than I.

**13** Lodge here tonight, and it shall be in the morning, if he will redeem you, good — let him redeem. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then I will redeem you, as the LORD lives. Lie down until the morning."

**14** And she lay at his feet until the morning, and she arose before one could recognize another. And he said, "Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor."

**15** And he said, "Bring the shawl that is upon you and hold it." And she held it, and he measured out six measures of barley and placed it upon her. And he went into the city.

**16** And she came to her mother-in-law, and she said, "How did it go with you, my daughter?" And she told her all that the man had done for her.

**17** And she said, "These six measures of barley he gave to me, for he said to me, 'Do not go empty-handed to your mother-in-law.'"

**18** And she said, "Sit still, my daughter, until you know how the matter will fall, for the man will not rest unless he has settled the matter today."

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **כְּנָפֶךָ (kenafekha), v. 9** — "Your wing," singular. Ruth's request deliberately echoes Boaz's own blessing in 2:12, where he prayed she would find refuge under God's "wings" (כְּנָפָיו). Now Ruth asks Boaz himself to be the instrument of that divine covering. The singular כָּנָף also means "corner of a garment," so Ruth is simultaneously asking Boaz to spread the corner of his garment over her — a gesture of betrothal.

2. **גֹּאֵל (go'el), vv. 9, 12, 13** — "Kinsman-redeemer," maintained consistently. Ruth names the legal institution directly, invoking Boaz's obligation and right.

3. **חֶסֶד (hesed), v. 10** — "Faithful love," here describing Ruth's action. Boaz identifies two acts of hesed: the first, her devotion to Naomi (ch. 1); the second, her coming to him rather than pursuing younger men.

4. **אֵשֶׁת חַיִל (eshet chayil), v. 11** — "Woman of worth," echoing the description of Boaz in 2:1 as אִישׁ גִּבּוֹר חַיִל. The same root (חַיִל) binds these two characters together as matching counterparts. This phrase reappears in Proverbs 31:10.

5. **שַׁעַר עַמִּי (sha'ar ammi), v. 11** — "The gate of my people," literally rendered. The gate was the civic center where legal proceedings occurred, so "all the gate" means "all the community" or "the whole civic body."

6. **וַיָּבֹא הָעִיר (vayyavo ha'ir), v. 15** — The Leningrad Codex reads "and he went into the city," with the masculine subject. Some manuscripts read "and she went," but the Codex clearly has the masculine form, which we follow.

— LOGOS
